result:
There is a specific kind of magic in holding a fossil. It is a portal, a tangible thread connecting the present to an unimaginable past. I have a small, unassuming ammonite on my desk. Its spiral is perfect, a mathematical masterpiece etched in stone, yet it was once a living, breathing creature navigating a prehistoric ocean. When I run my thumb along its ridged contours, I am touching a version of the Earth that existed tens of millions of years before the first human ever took a breath.

It is a humbling experience. We spend our days rushing from one task to the next, bound to clocks and calendars, convinced that our immediate concerns are the center of the universe. But a fossil renders that perspective absurd. It reminds us of deep time—that staggering, sweeping scale of history where millennia pass like seconds. The mountains rose and eroded, continents drifted, and entire species flourished and vanished, all while this little creature waited in the mud to be discovered.

There is comfort in this vastness. Our mistakes feel less monumental, our griefs less permanent, when measured against the slow, patient turning of the geological wheel. We are merely passing through, borrowing space and time for a fleeting moment. To hold a piece of the ancient world is to be reminded of our own transience, and to find a strange, beautiful peace in the knowledge that long after we are gone, the Earth will continue its quiet, enduring evolution.
